US10 – Layout: Tab / Tile / Overlay

Three concepts. "Overlay" = multiple metrics on one plot (e.g. pace + HR on the series chart). Click highlighted controls to see state changes.

Concept A

Three-way layout dial (Tab | 2-Split | 4-Grid) + overlay toggle within Series panel

The top bar gains a layout selector that is completely separate from the plot-type selector. Overlay lives in the Series panel controls, independent of layout.

Layout = Tab (current behaviour — one plot at a time)

StravaViz
Layout:
Filters
Run · last 12 mo
3:30–7:00 /km
Roster
London 2024
London 2023
Manchester
SCATTER Distance vs Pace · 847 activities
overlay: Pace ✕ HR Elev + add metric

Layout = Split — two panels, independently configured

StravaViz
Layout:
Filters
Run · last 12 mo
Roster
London 2024
London 2023
SCATTER
SERIES
Pace HR +

Layout = Grid — four panels 2×2

StravaViz
Layout:
Filters
Run · last 12 mo
Roster
London '24
London '23
SCATTER
scatter
SERIESHR
series
MAP
map
TABLE
table

Pros

  • ✓ Layout and plot-type are clearly separate concepts
  • ✓ Overlay stays in the Series panel header — no new global controls
  • ✓ 4-Grid gives true side-by-side-by-side-by-side comparison
  • ✓ Tabbed mode is identical to today — zero regression

Cons

  • ✗ Grid with 4 panels is cramped on smaller screens
  • ✗ Top bar is now busier (two segment groups)
  • ✗ Each panel needs its own plot-type switcher → more components

Interactions

  • • Layout dial is a top-level persistent preference (localStorage)
  • • In Split/Grid, each panel has its own plot-type dropdown
  • • Overlay chips appear only in Series panels
  • • "+" chip adds next available metric (HR → Elev → Cadence)
Concept B

Named preset layouts + metric stack within Series panel

No free-form tile configuration. Pick from five named presets ("Classic", "Race preview", "Race analysis", "Deep dive", "Overview"). Overlay is a reorderable metric stack inside the Series panel header.

Preset:
Single plot, one at a time (today's default)
StravaViz

Roster
London 24
SCATTER847 activities

scatter

StravaVizRace analysis
Roster
London 24
London 23
SERIES
Pace ✓ HR Elev +
MAP
route map
StravaVizDeep dive
Filters
Run · 12 mo
Roster
London 24
SERIES
series
TABLE
activity table
StravaVizOverview
Filters
Run
Roster
London 24
SCATTER
scatter
SERIES
series
MAP
map
TABLE
table

Pros

  • ✓ Opinionated presets remove decision fatigue
  • ✓ "Classic" is today's UI — no regression
  • ✓ Overlay chips are per-panel and composable
  • ✓ Preset name shows context of what you're doing

Cons

  • ✗ Users can't mix arbitrary combinations (e.g. scatter + table)
  • ✗ Preset list may grow unwieldy over time
  • ✗ In tiled presets, the plot-type tabs in top bar are meaningless / hidden

Interactions

  • • Preset buttons are the only layout control — no per-panel type switcher
  • • Overlay chips in Series panel header: click to enable, ✕ to remove
  • • Last used preset restored from localStorage on reload
Concept C

Minimal extension: current tabs + split-screen toggle + overlay inside Series controls

Least disruptive. Keep the existing tab bar unchanged. Add a small split icon that pins a second panel beside the active tab. Overlay piggybacks on the existing Y-metric system in SeriesPlot as a secondary-axis toggle.

Default: tabbed, no split

StravaViz
Filters
Run · 12 mo
Roster
London 24
London 23
SCATTER 847 activities
overlay: Pace ✕ HR Elev + add
MAP
route map

⊟ button above the chart area toggles the split pane. The second panel has its own plot-type dropdown. When in Series mode, overlay chips appear in the panel footer.

Pros

  • ✓ Minimal changes to existing code and layout
  • ✓ Tab bar and all existing muscle memory unchanged
  • ✓ Overlay is an additive change to SeriesPlot only
  • ✓ Split pane looks discoverable but not intrusive

Cons

  • ✗ Can only ever show max 2 panels simultaneously
  • ✗ No 4-panel option for true overview
  • ✗ Tab bar mode is ambiguous when split is active (which tab is "active"?)

Interactions

  • • ⊟ icon next to tabs opens/closes the split pane
  • • Split pane has its own plot-type dropdown (independent of tab bar)
  • • Overlay chips appear only when primary panel is Series
  • • Split state and second panel type persisted in localStorage

Comparison summary

Criterion A Dial B Presets C Minimal
Max simultaneous panels442
Overlay supportPer Series panelPer Series panelPer Series panel
Dashboard refactor scopeLarge — new panel modelMedium — preset routingSmall — additive only
Fits US18 (crosshair+map) use case?Yes — SplitYes — Race analysis presetYes — split pane
User configurabilityHigh (any combo)Medium (fixed presets)Low — but sufficient
Regression risk to current UXLow (tab dial = today)Lowest (Classic = today)Lowest (unchanged tabs)
Best suited if…Want full flexibility long-termHappy with curated workflowsWant minimal risk / smallest diff
Concept A — addendum

Overlay axis modes: Stacked vs Dual-axis

When multiple metrics are active in a Series panel, the chart can render them in two fundamentally different ways. Toggle below to compare.

Axis mode:

Each metric occupies a proportional band of the plot. Bands share the x-axis but have independent y-axes. Band heights are adjustable by dragging the divider.

Band split: 50% / 50% (drag handle in real implementation)
SERIES London 2024 · London 2023 · Manchester 2024  ·  X: distance
Pace ✕ Elevation ✕ HR +
Elevation (m)
⣿ drag
Pace (min/km)

Stacked pros

  • ✓ Y-axes never conflict — each metric has its own scale
  • ✓ Adjustable proportions let you emphasise what matters
  • ✓ Works cleanly with 3+ metrics (equal split or user-set)
  • ✓ Similar to Garmin Connect / Strava segment charts

Stacked cons

  • ✗ Hard to see correlation between metrics (e.g. pace vs HR) across bands
  • ✗ Each band needs enough height to be readable — cramped at small sizes
  • ✗ Drag handle interaction adds complexity

Best for

  • Elevation + pace — very different scales, interesting spatially
  • Cadence + pace — both running metrics but unrelated scales
  • When you want to visually scan each metric independently
SERIES London 2024 · London 2023  ·  X: distance
Pace ✗ (left) HR ✗ (right) +

Dual-axis pros

  • ✓ Compact — full height available for every metric
  • ✓ Correlation between metrics is immediately visible (e.g. pace slows, HR spikes)
  • ✓ Familiar pattern (financial charts, Garmin race overlays)

Dual-axis cons

  • ✗ 3+ metrics → 3+ y-axes → visual clutter
  • ✗ Solid vs dashed distinction is the only separator — can be hard to read
  • ✗ Y-axis scale manipulation can mislead (correlation looks stronger/weaker than it is)
  • ✗ Elevation as area fill doesn't work — must use a line

Best for

  • Pace + HR — classic run analysis pairing
  • When you want to spot where pace and effort diverge
  • Limited to 2 metrics for clearest results

Open design questions for implementation

  • 1. Default mode per pairing: should Pace+Elevation default to Stacked (scales incompatible) and Pace+HR default to Dual-axis (most useful correlation)?
  • 2. 3+ metrics in Dual-axis: cap at 2 metrics for dual-axis mode, or allow a third y-axis pinned to the left (pace) and two on the right?
  • 3. 3+ metrics in Stacked: equal splits by default, or does the first metric always get 50% and extras split the remainder?
  • 4. Persisting split proportions: per-session (sessionStorage) or per-panel-config (localStorage with LayoutConfig)?
  • 5. Axis lock: when two activities share a stacked band, should their y-scales be locked together or independent?
